title: Unexpected Performance on Quadrature Direct-Digital-Frequency-Synthesizer Example
body:
Overview: working example of Verilator is unexpectedly slow. I don't expect an easy resolution of this, this posting is mostly for informational purposes.
Background: The example was originally developed using Dolphin's Smash simulator, an excellent mixed-mode simulator that unfortunately is no longer supported. The example is completely verilog at the gate level with timing, except in Smash, the CLK and INIT signals are analog (I could change this - but it's not very relevant to this post). I am a Verilator newbie; the Verilator example is working as expected (see below). The example is a Direct Digital Frequency Synthesizer having quadrature outputs (12 bits each). It is based on using ROM lookup tables (two being 256x12bits and two being 256x4 bits; i.e. small and fast). It is a hierarchical design realized at the gate level and has extensive pipe-lining. Each gate has its timing matched to Spice simulated delays of a generic 22nm process (about +- 15% accuracy). The design contains 454 different subcircuits, 8278 verilog processes, 185,665 logic ports; I would consider this medium to small complexity. As mentioned, the design simulates as expected in Smash, and was ported to Verilator. I was hoping this would result in much faster simulation times (so I could use Verilator to minimize frequency spurs). The opposite is the case. I also verified the design using iVerilog which is simulating much faster than both Dolphin (as expected) and much much faster than Verilator which was not expected. In Dolphin, a normal simulation might be 33,600 clock periods. The reported simulations times are for only 65 clock periods, the maximum I can simulate in Verilator. The simulation is running at 1GHz (it easily runs at 2.5GHz clock speed, but I didn't want speed to be an issue).
Results using bash's "time" function (elapsed times - Smash is running on an old computer due to node-locked license, Verilator and iVerilog are running on a new computer - 12th Gen Intel® Core™ i9-12900K × 24 - 64.0 GiB running Ubuntu 22.04.2) (for iVerilog and Verilator, vcd is being dumped for 3 top layers - I'm guessing elapsed time doesn't include vcd dumping time at least for iVerilog):
Smash: 11.26s
iVerilog: 1.8s (note: changing to 3360 periods gives the same time - actual simulation time must be just a small percentage). I'm guessing iVerilog is not interpreted as stated?
Verilator: 247 minutes, 13.18s, changing the number of periods larger results in much longer elapsed times.
In all three cases, the simulation results as verified using gtkwave and by storing the quadrature outputs into a file. The results appear to be correct (as expected - a short segment from the file are shown below for the synthesizer funning at exactly Fclk/16) and all three cases give exactly the same results.
Question: could it be Verilator has problems simulating in reasonable time with timing and "specify" statements?
Because Verilator is taking so long to simulate; debugging this might be real time consuming; I could run a few different scenarios over night? Using htop, it appears Verilator is running on only one core.
Short segment from output file (identical for all three cases:
    0 -2048
  783 -1892
 1447 -1449
 1891  1264
 2047     0
 1891   783
 1448  1447
-1265  1891
   -1  2047
 -784  1891
-1448  1448
-1892 -1265
-2048    -1
-1892  -784
-1449 -1448
 1264 -1892
    0 -2048
